title: Why Your Money Story Controls Your Financial Future (And How to Rewrite It)
guestBio: Stephanie Guttman is the founder of Huntress Wealth and host of the Talk Rich to Me podcast. She specializes in helping women overcome limiting money beliefs and close the investing gap through accessible financial education and strategies.
excerpt: Your relationship with money was shaped before you could even read—and it might be sabotaging your financial future. Discover how to identify and rewrite the money story that's been running your life.
metaDescription: Learn how to identify your money story formed by age 7 and why rewriting it is key to closing the investing gap for women.
keyTakeaways: Your money story is formed by age 7 and falls into four categories: avoidance, money worship, status, or vigilance.
The investing gap costs women more financially than the wage gap over a lifetime.
ETFs make investing accessible without requiring complex financial knowledge or fancy strategies.
Money invested in the market doubles approximately every 10 years through compound interest.
Talking to children about investing before age 16 triples their financial confidence as adults.
Identifying whether your money story helps or hurts you is the first step to financial transformation.
Starting to invest early, even with small amounts, has more impact than waiting to invest larger sums later.

pullQuotes.text: Your money story was written before you could read. What if it's time for a rewrite?
The investing gap costs women more than the wage gap
Talking to kids about investing before 16 triples their financial confidence

timestamps.label: The Four Money Stories Formed by Age 7
Why the Investing Gap Matters More Than the Wage Gap
Teaching Children About Money and Investing

content:
What if I told you that your entire relationship with money—every financial decision you make, every investment you avoid, every moment of money stress you experience—was predetermined before you turned seven years old? It sounds impossible, but according to financial expert Stephanie Guttman, founder of Huntress Wealth, this is exactly what's happening to most of us.

In this eye-opening conversation with Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her podcast, Stephanie reveals how our earliest experiences with money create invisible scripts that run our financial lives for decades. More importantly, she shows us how to rewrite those scripts to finally build the wealth we deserve.

The Four Money Stories That Rule Your Life

By age seven, you've already absorbed one of four distinct money stories from watching the adults around you. Stephanie identifies these as avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance. Each story creates a different set of financial behaviors that can either propel you forward or hold you back.

The avoidance story teaches you that money is stressful, complicated, or somehow dirty. If this is your story, you might find yourself putting off financial decisions, avoiding investment conversations, or feeling overwhelmed by anything money-related. The money worship story, on the other hand, suggests that money is the solution to all problems and the source of happiness. This can lead to workaholism, overspending, or tying your self-worth to your net worth.

The status story focuses on money as a symbol of success and comparison tool. If this resonates with you, you might find yourself making financial decisions based on what others will think rather than what serves your actual goals. Finally, the vigilance story teaches that money is scarce and must be hoarded or protected at all costs. While this can create excellent savers, it can also prevent people from taking the calculated risks necessary to build real wealth.

The crucial insight here is that none of these stories are inherently good or bad—they're simply frameworks that may or may not serve you in your current life. The key is developing awareness of which story is running your financial decisions and whether it's helping you build the life you actually want.

Why the Investing Gap Matters More Than the Wage Gap

While much attention rightfully focuses on the wage gap between men and women, Stephanie argues that the investing gap may actually cost women more over their lifetimes. Women are significantly less likely to invest than men, often keeping their money in savings accounts where it barely keeps pace with inflation rather than putting it to work in the market where it can grow substantially over time.

This gap isn't just about financial knowledge—it's about confidence, risk tolerance, and those deep-seated money stories we absorbed in childhood. Women are often socialized to be more risk-averse and to prioritize security over growth, which can translate into missing out on decades of compound returns.

The math is startling when you consider that money invested in diversified market funds typically doubles every ten years. A woman who starts investing at 25 versus 35 doesn't just get ten extra years of returns—she gets the exponential benefit of those returns compounding over time. That ten-year head start can literally translate to hundreds of thousands of dollars in additional wealth by retirement.

Making Investing Accessible for Every Woman

One of the most empowering aspects of Stephanie's approach is her insistence that successful investing doesn't require fancy strategies, complex analysis, or insider knowledge. Instead, she advocates for simple, low-cost ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) that allow you to own small pieces of hundreds or thousands of companies with a single purchase.

This approach removes many of the barriers that keep women from investing. You don't need to research individual stocks, time the market, or have thousands of dollars to get started. Many brokerages now allow you to start investing with just a few dollars, and ETFs provide instant diversification that would be impossible for individual investors to achieve on their own.

The beauty of this strategy is that it removes emotion and complexity from the equation. Instead of trying to pick winning stocks or predict market movements, you're simply betting on the long-term growth of the overall economy—a bet that has paid off consistently over decades of market history.

The Compound Interest Secret

Perhaps the most powerful concept Stephanie shares is the magic of compound interest—what Einstein allegedly called the eighth wonder of the world. The idea that your money doubles every ten years in the market isn't just theory; it's historical fact based on long-term market performance.

This means that $10,000 invested at age 25 becomes $20,000 by 35, $40,000 by 45, $80,000 by 55, and $160,000 by 65—without adding a single additional dollar. When you do continue adding money regularly, the numbers become even more dramatic. This is why starting early matters so much more than starting with large amounts.

Teaching the Next Generation

One of the most practical insights from this conversation is Stephanie's research showing that children who learn about investing before age 16 have three times the financial confidence as adults. This isn't about turning your kids into day traders—it's about normalizing conversations about money growth and helping them understand that money can work for them rather than something they always have to work for.

As working mothers and leaders, we have the opportunity to rewrite not just our own money stories but also the ones we're passing down to our children. This might mean having age-appropriate conversations about investing, modeling healthy financial behaviors, or simply being more open about money decisions in your household.

Your Permission to Rewrite

The most powerful message from this episode is that you have permission to question and rewrite the money story that was handed to you in childhood. Just because you learned that investing was risky, that you're not good with money, or that wealth building is too complicated doesn't mean those beliefs have to define your financial future.

Rewriting your money story starts with awareness—identifying which of the four patterns resonates most with you and honestly assessing whether it's serving your current goals. From there, it's about taking small, concrete actions that align with the financial future you want to create rather than the limitations you inherited.

Whether you're a mother wanting to model healthy money habits, a leader ready to take control of your financial future, or someone who's been avoiding financial planning for too long, remember that it's never too late to start writing a new chapter. Your seven-year-old self did the best she could with the information available, but you don't have to let her money fears or beliefs limit your adult potential.
